input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator** designed for temperature regulation within an insulated container. The system operates in the **thermal control domain**, with the primary goal of maintaining a predefined temperature inside a styrofoam box regardless of its contents. Key components include:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to minimize external environmental influences.
  - **Heating Element**: A heatbed responsible for generating and distributing heat inside the box.
  - **Fan**: A mechanical component for uniform temperature distribution within the enclosed space.
  - **Temperature Sensors**: Two sensors monitor the **internal temperature** of the box, while one sensor tracks the **external ambient temperature** for environmental context.
  The system is controlled by a **Raspberry Pi**, acting as the embedded computational unit for real-time monitoring and actuation. The PT is representative of **CPSs** due to its integration of physical processes (thermal dynamics) with computational elements (control logic), making it suitable for **Digital Twin (DT) implementation** to enhance predictability, safety, and operational robustness.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous acquisition of **sensor data** (internal/external temperatures) from the PT, enabling **state estimation** and **anomaly detection** (e.g., via fault detectors).
  - **Predictive Simulation**: Execution of **dynamic models** (e.g., differential equation-based simulations) to **reproduce and predict** the PT’s behavior under current or hypothetical operating conditions, accounting for forces (e.g., thermal gradients) and environmental changes.
  - **Calibration & Model Refinement**: Use of **calibration algorithms** to update **cyber-physical models** (e.g., thermal transfer models) with real-time data, ensuring alignment between the DT and PT. This includes **parameter estimation** for accuracy improvements.
  - **Safety & Optimisation**: Deployment of **supervisory controllers** and **optimisers** to evaluate system performance against safety thresholds and operational objectives. Violations trigger **corrective actions** (e.g., adjustments to heating/fan control) via human or automated decision-making.
  - **Visualisation & Communication**: Generation of **advanced visualisations** (e.g., thermal distribution maps, control signal trends) to support **engineering communication**, change impact analysis, and **reconfigurability** for robustness in dynamic environments.
  Target users include **engineers, operators, and maintenance personnel**, with services tailored for **predictive maintenance, fault diagnosis, and adaptive control**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** for the incubator leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, as inferred from the case study and general DT frameworks:
  - **Hardware Platform**: **Raspberry Pi** for embedded control and data acquisition, interfacing with sensors and actuators (heatbed, fan).
  - **Modelling & Simulation**:
    - **Dynamic Models**: Mathematical representations (e.g., **differential equations**) describing thermal dynamics, calibrated via **parameter estimation algorithms**.
    - **Simulation Engines**: Likely **Python-based** (e.g., SciPy, SimPy) or **Modelica**-compatible tools for executing high-fidelity simulations of the PT’s behavior.
  - **Data Acquisition & Storage**:
    - **Sensor Data**: Real-time temperature readings from **two internal sensors** and **one external sensor**, transmitted to the DT for processing.
    - **Databases**: Lightweight or time-series databases (e.g., **InfluxDB, SQLite**) to store historical sensor data and model parameters for calibration.
  - **Algorithmic Components**:
    - **Calibration Algorithms**: Techniques (e.g., **least-squares optimization, Bayesian inference**) to refine model parameters using sensor data.
    - **Fault Detection**: **Supervisory controllers** (e.g., PID-based or model-predictive control) and **state estimators** (e.g., Kalman filters) for real-time monitoring.
    - **Optimisation Tools**: Algorithms (e.g., **gradient descent, genetic algorithms**) to optimise control policies or energy consumption.
  - **Software Framework**: The **INTO-CPS Association’s example incubator repository** ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) provides a **reference implementation** with hardware/software specifications, likely using **Python, ROS (Robot Operating System), or MATLAB/Simulink** for integration.
  - **Visualisation Tools**: Custom or open-source tools (e.g., **Matplotlib, Plotly, or Unity-based dashboards**) for rendering simulation results and real-time telemetry.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study does not explicitly detail multiplicities or hierarchical structures in the provided documents. However, the system is designed as a **centralized Digital Twin framework** integrating a single **Physical Twin (PT)**—the incubator—with its corresponding **Cyber Twin (CT)**. The architecture employs **calibration algorithms** to update models of the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** and environment in real-time, implying a **one-to-one mapping** between the PT and its DT instance. No decentralized or federated architectures, multiple DT instances, or explicit coordination mechanisms between twins are described. The scope of responsibility is confined to the **incubator’s temperature regulation system**, with no indication of distributed or modular DT instances beyond the core CPS components (e.g., sensors, heatbed, fan).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system for the incubator follows a **closed-loop orchestration architecture** centered on **model-based monitoring, simulation, and optimization**. The constellation integrates three core components:
  1. **Data Acquisition**: Real-time sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperature sensors) from the **Physical Twin (PT)** is streamed to the **Cyber Twin (CT)**.
  2. **Model Calibration**: **Calibration algorithms** dynamically adjust the **CPS and environmental models** (e.g., thermal dynamics of the styrofoam box, heatbed behavior) using the incoming data to minimize parameter estimation errors. This ensures the DT’s predictive accuracy aligns with the PT’s current state.
  3. **Monitoring & Control**: The calibrated models feed into **monitors** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers) that evaluate system performance against safety and operational constraints. Violations trigger **human-in-the-loop or automated interventions** (e.g., adjusting the heatbed or fan) to maintain the target temperature.
  The architecture leverages **feedback loops** between the PT and CT, with no explicit mention of **event-driven, microservices, or service-oriented patterns**. Resource management is implicit, focusing on **real-time data processing** and **model fidelity** rather than distributed orchestration. The system’s coordination is **tightly coupled** to the incubator’s CPS, with no external orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, Apache Kafka) referenced.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent the behavior of the Physical Twin (PT) and its environment. **Model types** include:
  - **Geometric/Structural Models**: Abstract representations of physical components (e.g., CAD models of a car’s chassis, wheels, or an incubator’s insulated box), capturing static attributes like dimensions and shapes.
  - **Behavioral/Dynamic Models**: Mathematical formulations (e.g., differential equations) describing system dynamics, such as forces acting on a car or thermal inertia in an incubator’s heatbed. In the incubator case study, two simulation models were developed: one with **two free parameters** (simpler) and another with **four free parameters** (more accurate for thermal inertia prediction).
  - **Analytical Models**: Models derived from first-principles physics or empirical data, used for predictive simulations (e.g., temperature prediction inside the incubator).
  **Data sources** originate from the PT via **sensors** (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator) and are collected **over time** to reflect real-world conditions. **Data management** involves:
  - **Calibration algorithms** to update model parameters (e.g., adjusting free parameters in the incubator’s thermal model) using real-time sensor data to minimize prediction errors.
  - **Integration with monitoring systems** (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) to validate model accuracy and system performance.
  **Model relationships** are hierarchical: lower-fidelity models (e.g., 2-parameter incubator model) may serve as baselines, while higher-fidelity models (e.g., 4-parameter model) refine predictions. The DT framework leverages these models and data to enable **simulation, prediction, and optimization** of the PT’s behavior under current or hypothetical operating conditions. The incubator case study demonstrates open-source hardware/software for model development and verification, emphasizing reproducibility and extensibility.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin (DT) relies on a **sensing infrastructure** to monitor the Physical Twin (PT) and its environment. Key components include:
  - **Sensor Types**: In the incubator case study, **temperature sensors** are explicitly mentioned to measure the internal thermal environment. Other implied sensors (not detailed) may include environmental sensors (e.g., humidity, ambient temperature) or structural sensors (e.g., strain gauges in mechanical systems like cars).
  - **Measurement Capabilities**: Sensors provide **real-time data** (e.g., temperature readings inside the incubator) to calibrate and validate models. The incubator’s thermal models depend on sensor data to adjust **free parameters** (2 or 4) for improved fidelity.
  - **Spatial Distribution**: Sensors are strategically placed to capture critical variables (e.g., multiple temperature sensors in the incubator to account for spatial thermal gradients).
  - **Sampling Frequencies/Data Transmission Rates**: Not explicitly specified in the documents, but implied to be **high enough to support real-time monitoring and calibration** (e.g., continuous or near-continuous sampling for dynamic systems like incubators or cars).
  - **Accuracy and Precision**: The incubator case study highlights that the **4-parameter model** (using sensor data) better predicts thermal inertia, suggesting high-precision sensors are required. No quantitative specifications (e.g., ±0.1°C) are provided.
  - **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Sensor data is transmitted to the DT’s **cyber component** (e.g., embedded computers in the incubator’s software system) via **communication protocols** (e.g., Controller Area Network (CAN) in cars or proprietary protocols in incubators). The data is then processed by **calibration algorithms** to update models dynamically.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin (DT) emphasizes **model fidelity** and **validity** through structured methodologies:
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements**: Higher-fidelity models (e.g., the incubator’s **4-parameter thermal model**) are preferred when greater accuracy is needed (e.g., predicting complex dynamics like thermal inertia). Lower-fidelity models (e.g., **2-parameter model**) may suffice for simpler use cases or preliminary analysis.
  - **Validation and Verification Methods**: The incubator case study demonstrates **experimental validation** by comparing model predictions (e.g., temperature trajectories) against real-world sensor data. Models are iteratively refined until predictions align with observed behavior.
  - **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**: Uncertainties arise from:
    - **Sensor inaccuracies** (e.g., noise in temperature readings).
    - **Model simplifications** (e.g., ignoring minor thermal effects in the 2-parameter model).
    - **Environmental variability** (e.g., external temperature fluctuations affecting the incubator).
    Quantification is implicit in the incubator study, where the **4-parameter model’s superior performance** suggests reduced uncertainty in thermal inertia predictions.
  - **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**: The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to adjust model parameters (e.g., free parameters in the incubator’s thermal model) using real-time sensor data. **Monitors** (e.g., fault detectors) identify deviations between predicted and actual PT behavior, triggering corrective actions (e.g., controller adjustments or human intervention).
  - **Quality Assurance Processes**: The incubator case study adheres to **open-source principles**, ensuring reproducibility and transparency. Models are developed with **experimental verification**, and the DT framework integrates **closed-loop validation** (e.g., comparing simulation outputs to sensor data during operation). For broader systems (e.g., cars), quality assurance may include **standardized testing protocols** (e.g., ISO/SAE guidelines for automotive DTs) and **model certification** processes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system receives **real-time sensor data** from the Physical Twin (PT) via **two internal temperature sensors** and **one external temperature sensor**, all of which monitor the thermal environment inside and outside the insulated styrofoam box. The data types transmitted include **analog temperature readings** (likely in °C or °F) with **high temporal resolution** to enable dynamic model calibration. The communication protocol is not explicitly specified in the documents, but the system implies a **low-latency, bidirectional data exchange** between the PT (Raspberry Pi-based controller) and the DT. **Event triggers** for data transmission are likely tied to **sensor polling intervals** or **threshold-based alerts** (e.g., temperature deviations). **Data quality assurance** is inferred to involve **calibration algorithms** that update the DT’s models (e.g., differential equations or physics-based simulations) with the best estimate of parameters, ensuring alignment between the PT and its virtual counterpart. **Preprocessing steps** may include **filtering, normalization, or noise reduction** before integration into the DT’s data pipeline, though specifics are not detailed. **Data integration mechanisms** involve the DT’s **monitoring components**, which use the calibrated data to assess system performance and detect violations (e.g., unsafe or suboptimal thermal conditions).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) exerts **control commands** back to the Physical Twin (PT) via the **Raspberry Pi-based controller**, which executes actions to regulate the incubator’s temperature. The **command types** include **setpoint adjustments** (e.g., target temperature modifications) and **actuator control signals** (e.g., activating/deactivating the heatbed or fan). The **control message format** is not explicitly defined, but it likely adheres to a **structured protocol** (e.g., MQTT, Modbus, or custom TCP/IP messages) to ensure compatibility with the PT’s embedded system. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not detailed, though the DT’s **monitoring components** (e.g., fault detectors or supervisory controllers) may enforce **safety checks** before command execution. **Feedback systems** are implied through the **closed-loop control architecture**, where the PT’s sensor data is continuously fed back to the DT for recalibration and optimization. **Conflict resolution** may occur if the DT detects **violation conditions** (e.g., temperature overshoot), triggering **corrective actions** (e.g., throttling the heatbed or adjusting fan speed). **Command execution confirmation** is likely achieved via **acknowledgment signals** from the PT (e.g., status updates or sensor validation), though the exact mechanism is unspecified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system relies on a **cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture** where the **Raspberry Pi** serves as the primary **gateway node** for data and command exchange between the PT and DT. The **network topology** is **star-based**, with the Raspberry Pi acting as the central hub connecting sensors (temperature probes) and actuators (heatbed, fan). The **communication protocols** are not explicitly stated, but the system likely employs **lightweight, low-latency protocols** (e.g., **MQTT, CoAP, or direct GPIO/ADC interfaces**) due to the embedded nature of the PT. **Bandwidth requirements** are minimal, as the data payloads consist primarily of **low-frequency sensor readings** (e.g., <1Hz polling rate for temperature sensors). **Latency requirements** are critical for real-time control, though exact thresholds are unspecified. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not detailed, but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** implicitly handle **data inconsistencies** by refining model parameters. **Security protocols** for network communication are not mentioned, though the DT’s **monitoring and control components** may include **basic integrity checks** (e.g., validating sensor ranges or command plausibility) to mitigate cyber-physical risks.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context includes the following phases: **(1) Modeling Phase**: Abstract representations of the Physical Twin (PT) are created at varying levels of abstraction (e.g., CAD models for geometric properties or dynamic models described by differential equations for behavioral analysis). These models may include cyber-physical system (CPS) components and environmental interactions. **(2) Integration Phase**: Models are integrated into the DT framework alongside real-time data streams from sensors and algorithms (e.g., calibration algorithms, fault detectors, supervisory controllers). **(3) Deployment Phase**: The DT is deployed alongside the PT, enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and optimization. **(4) Operational Phase**: Continuous calibration and validation occur via data-driven updates (e.g., parameter estimation for models) to maintain accuracy. **(5) Evolution Phase**: The DT adapts to changes in the PT or environment, with human or algorithmic interventions triggered by violations detected in monitors (e.g., safety or performance deviations). Transition management between phases relies on **calibration algorithms** to reconcile model parameters with real-world data, ensuring consistency. Lifecycle governance is implicit through the iterative feedback loop between the PT and DT, where deviations prompt corrective actions (e.g., reconfiguration of the PT or model updates).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process for the Digital Twin (DT) in the incubator case study follows a structured methodology involving **(1) Hardware Specification**: The Physical Twin (PT) is defined by components such as an insulated styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and temperature sensors (two internal, one external). **(2) Model Development**: Two distinct models are built—one for the CPS and another for the environment—each incorporating calibration methods to align with real-world data. **(3) Algorithm Integration**: Algorithms (e.g., calibration, fault detection, supervisory control) process sensor data to generate actionable insights, such as parameter updates for models or control adjustments. **(4) Validation**: Experimental results (Section 4) validate the DT’s accuracy, with simulations reproducing PT behavior under current conditions. **(5) Evolution Management**: The DT evolves via iterative calibration, where discrepancies between simulated and real-world data trigger model refinements. **Quality Assurance (QA)** is implied through empirical validation (e.g., temperature regulation experiments) and adherence to the PT’s operational constraints. **Version Control** is not explicitly detailed but inferred from the iterative refinement process, where updated models replace older versions based on performance metrics. The process leverages **Raspberry Pi** for control and a **public repository** ([INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) for hardware/software specifications, ensuring reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin (DT) generates insights through **(1) Real-Time Monitoring**: Sensor data (e.g., internal/external temperatures) is continuously collected to track PT performance. **(2) Simulation-Based Predictions**: Models simulate PT behavior under current conditions, enabling **predictive analytics** (e.g., temperature dynamics) and **what-if analyses** (e.g., impact of environmental changes). **(3) Anomaly Detection**: Monitors (e.g., supervisory controllers) identify deviations from safe/optimal operation, triggering alerts for human or algorithmic intervention. **(4) Decision Support**: Insights are used to **(a) Reconfigure the PT** (e.g., adjust heating/fan settings), **(b) Update Models** via calibration algorithms, or **(c) Optimize Maintenance Schedules**. **User Interaction** occurs through visualizations (e.g., schematic overviews in Fig. 1/2) and experimental results (Section 4), where decisions are validated against empirical data. **Confidence Measures** are implicit in the calibration process, where model accuracy is quantified by the alignment between simulated and real-world outcomes (e.g., temperature regulation precision).

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller

Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Container
